title: Security-critical BFF auth proxy has zero test coverage and is undiscoverable by the vitest config
impact: The component that enforces 'no valid session -> 401', forwards the raw session token as a Bearer credential, streams/caps the upload body (413), and converts upstream failures to 502 is entirely unverified. A regression that drops the session check, forwards an attacker-controlled Authorization header, or leaks the body cap would pass CI green. Because vitest.config.ts only globs lib/**, a developer who adds app/api/.../route.test.ts would find it silently NOT run — the config actively prevents covering this path.
likelihood: Medium — this file is edited whenever proxying/auth behavior changes, and there is no guardrail; the include glob makes accidental no-coverage the default for any app/ test.
remediation: Broaden vitest include to e.g. ["{lib,app}/**/*.test.ts"] and add tests for proxy(): (1) no session -> 401 and upstream fetch is never called; (2) valid session -> Authorization: Bearer <session.token> is forwarded and inbound Authorization/cookie are not blindly trusted; (3) content-length over MAX_BODY_BYTES -> 413 without buffering; (4) upstream throw -> 502; (5) Retry-After passthrough. Mock auth.api.getSession and global fetch.
references: OWASP A01:2021 Broken Access Control; CWE-862 Missing Authorization; CWE-1230 (testing) gap on auth enforcement

title: Stall-recovery timeout (5 min) is shorter than a healthy job's worst-case runtime (~10 min), so recovery re-queues still-running jobs → duplicate OpenAI extraction / double charge
evidence: settings.py: PDF_PARSE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=60.0; EXTRACTION_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=180.0; EXTRACTION_MAX_ATTEMPTS=3; EXTRACTION_BACKOFF_BASE_SECONDS=2.0; WORKER_STALL_TIMEOUT_MINUTES=5. _run_operation_with_retries: `for attempt in range(1, self.max_attempts + 1): return await asyncio.wait_for(operation(), timeout=self.extraction_timeout) ... backoff = self.backoff_base * (2 ** (attempt - 1)); await asyncio.sleep(backoff)`. recover_stalled_jobs SQL: `WHERE status = 'processing' AND started_at < now() - make_interval(mins => timeout_minutes)`. started_at is set once at claim (claim_next_job: `started_at = now()`).
impact: A single legitimately slow job that retries transient OpenAI errors can run up to ~60s (parse) + 180+180+180s (3 attempts) + 2s + 4s (backoff) = ~600s, which is double the 300s stall window. recover_stalled_jobs then flips its row back to 'pending' WHILE the original worker is still mid-extraction; another worker (or another replica) claims it (attempts bumped) and runs the full extraction again — a second paid OpenAI call for the same document. The content-hash cache cannot prevent this because the first run hasn't completed yet, so find_cached_result returns nothing. The expected_attempts terminal-write guard correctly prevents a double DB write, but NOT the double spend. The design doc (docs/design.md:158-161) explicitly assumes recovery fires only 'well beyond a healthy job', which the default math contradicts. There is no heartbeat/lease renewal, so a long-but-healthy job cannot signal liveness to defer recovery.
likelihood: Medium — requires a job whose total runtime crosses 5 minutes, which happens whenever OpenAI is degraded (429/5xx/timeouts triggering retries) or on large vision-fallback PDFs. OpenAI degradation is exactly when retries (and thus long runtimes) are most likely, so the failure correlates with the trigger condition.
remediation: Make the stall timeout strictly greater than the worst-case healthy runtime: derive/validate WORKER_STALL_TIMEOUT_MINUTES >= ceil((PDF_PARSE_TIMEOUT + EXTRACTION_MAX_ATTEMPTS*EXTRACTION_TIMEOUT + sum(backoffs)) / 60) with margin (here ~12-15 min), or add a periodic 'processing' heartbeat (UPDATE started_at/last_heartbeat) that the recovery gate checks instead of the fixed claim time. Document the invariant and add a startup assertion so a future change to either timeout cannot silently re-introduce the overlap.
references: Crash-recovery lease must exceed max job duration; CWE-662 (improper synchronization) / cost-idempotency. docs/design.md:154-161

title: GET /jobs LIST embeds full records[]/flagged[] JSON of every job; dashboard polls it every 2.5s pulling up to 100 full jobs to render 5 cards
location: backend/app/api/schema/job.py:70-83 (from_job); backend/app/api/routes/jobs.py:106-123 (list_jobs default limit=100); frontend/lib/use-jobs.ts:49-65 (refresh + 2.5s poll); frontend/lib/api.ts:67-78 (listJobs called with no limit)
evidence: JobResponse.from_job: `records=result.get("records", []), flagged=result.get("flagged", [])` — the LIST route returns the SAME envelope as GET /jobs/{id}, so every list row carries the entire extraction result. useJobs.refresh: `setJobs(await listJobs())` with no args, and listJobs() sends no limit so the backend default `limit: int = Query(100, ...)` applies. The dashboard renders only `recent = jobs.slice(0, RECENT_LIMIT)` (5) but the poll re-fetches all 100 full job bodies every 2.5s: `const id = setInterval(() => void refresh(), 2500)`.
impact: A multi-page billing PDF can yield dozens of records; 100 jobs x (records[] + flagged[]) is easily multiple MB of JSON serialized, transferred through the BFF proxy, and JSON-parsed on every 2.5s tick — to show 5 summary cards. For a moderately active user (~100 completed docs, ~20 records each = ~2000 record objects) each poll ships ~1-3 MB. With many concurrent dashboards this saturates the API event loop on JSON serialization and the proxy on bytes. Break point: payload grows linearly with records-per-job and is unbounded by the 100-row cap because each row's size is unbounded.
likelihood: High — happens for every logged-in user on the default dashboard as soon as they have completed jobs; the polling makes it continuous.
remediation: Add a lightweight list projection: a JobSummaryResponse that omits records[]/flagged[] (or returns only counts) for GET /jobs, and fetch the full result only via GET /jobs/{id} when a row is expanded. For the dashboard recents, request `listJobs({ limit: RECENT_LIMIT })` instead of the default 100 so the poll pulls 5 rows, not 100.
references: OWASP API3:2023 Excessive Data Exposure (analogous); general N+1/over-fetch

title: GET /jobs/summary runs two un-indexed aggregate scans (jsonb_array_elements over all completed jobs) on every dashboard poll (every 3s while pending/processing)
location: backend/app/dao/pg/job_dao.py:203-268 (summary); frontend/lib/use-jobs.ts:104-113 (useSummary polls every 3000ms); backend/alembic/versions/f6b9c2d3e4a7_job_query_indexes.py (no index covers this query)
evidence: Financial query: `FROM (SELECT result FROM jobs WHERE status='completed' AND jsonb_typeof(result->'records')='array') j CROSS JOIN LATERAL jsonb_array_elements(j.result->'records') AS rec` plus five `SUM((rec->>'...')::numeric)`. Counts query does `SUM(jsonb_array_length(result->'records'))` over ALL rows. No expression/GIN index exists (grep of alembic shows only idx_jobs_owner_created and idx_jobs_pending_created). useSummary: `const id = setInterval(() => void refreshSummary(), 3000)` whenever processing/pending > 0.
impact: Both queries are a full RLS-scoped scan of the user's jobs that must deserialize and unnest every record JSON array on every call. There is no caching and no covering index, so cost is O(total records across all the user's completed jobs) per request. While any job is processing the dashboard re-runs this every 3s. Break point: a power user with thousands of completed records pays a multi-hundred-ms-to-second JSONB unnest per poll; many such users concurrently will pin Postgres CPU and exhaust the small pool (size 5 + overflow 10). This is the most expensive query in the system and it is on a polling hot path.
likelihood: High at scale — fires continuously during any active extraction, for every user whose history is large.
remediation: Cache the summary (short TTL, e.g. 5-15s, or invalidate on job completion) instead of recomputing per poll; or maintain denormalized per-job aggregate columns (records_count, summed financials) written once when the worker completes a job, so summary() becomes a plain SUM over indexed scalar columns. At minimum, decouple the polled 'is anything processing' check from the expensive financial roll-up so the financial scan isn't re-run every 3s.
references: OWASP API4:2023 Unrestricted Resource Consumption; CWE-1050 (excessive platform resource consumption)

title: Submission packager ships untracked, non-ignored artifacts (incl. PHI-derived eval output) via `git ls-files --others`
category: Data privacy / HIPAA hygiene (CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information)
evidence: _git_files() runs `git ls-files --cached --others --exclude-standard -z` (line 38-41) — `--others` includes ALL untracked files, gated only by gitignore (`--exclude-standard`) and a hand-maintained BLOCKED_PREFIXES tuple (line 20-31): (".env", "backend/pdfs/", "backend/app/.logs/", "frontend/node_modules/", ... "dist/"). Neither `test-results/` nor `tmp/` is in BLOCKED_PREFIXES, and neither is in .gitignore. Verified: `git ls-files --cached --others --exclude-standard` currently returns `test-results/.last-run.json`. README.md:151 instructs `python backend/scripts/evaluate_extraction.py --predictions tmp/predictions`, and `git check-ignore tmp` returns nothing — so eval output (structured billing records derived from PHI PDFs) lands in an untracked, non-ignored, non-blocklisted dir.
impact: After a developer runs the documented eval harness, re-running create_submission.py silently packages tmp/predictions/* — extracted billing records derived from PHI — into the submission zip. Same path leaks any other untracked scratch artifact (Playwright traces/screenshots that can contain rendered PHI). The allowlist-by-blocklist over untracked files fails open: every new artifact ships unless someone remembers to extend BLOCKED_PREFIXES. The current dist zip is clean only because it was built before these dirs existed.
likelihood: Medium — requires a re-pack after generating eval/test artifacts, which the README itself walks the user through; the failure is silent (no warning, the post-zip blocked-path check uses the same incomplete blocklist).
remediation: Package tracked files only (`git ls-files --cached`, drop `--others`), or switch to an explicit allowlist of source paths. At minimum add `tmp/` and `test-results/` to both .gitignore and BLOCKED_PREFIXES, and make the post-build verifier scan for data dirs (data/, tmp/, test-results/, *.pdf, *_gt.json) independent of the blocklist.
references: OWASP A01:2021 / CWE-200; HIPAA 164.312(a) minimum-necessary

title: Dashboard overview and Records pages have no <h1>; heading hierarchy starts at <h2>
category: WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A); WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels (AA)
evidence:
DashboardShell renders
/
with no

. The first heading produced by DashboardClient is an h2: `

Upload billing PDFs

` (dashboard-client.tsx:166); the four StatCards above it carry no heading at all. Records page is the same: ReportSection's first heading is `

Records report

` (report-section.tsx:110). By contrast the Documents page (documents-client.tsx:25 `

All documents

`) and the job-detail page (job-detail-client.tsx:118 `

`) do provide an h1.
impact: Screen-reader users navigating by heading land on these two primary pages with no top-level (h1) heading and a hierarchy that begins at h2, so they cannot identify the page's main topic or get a correct document outline. Inconsistent with the other dashboard pages that do have an h1.
likelihood: Certain on every visit to /dashboard and /dashboard/records by any heading-navigation AT user (a primary navigation strategy for blind users).
remediation:
Add a visually appropriate (or sr-only)

as the first heading of each page's content — e.g. an

Overview

/

Records

in DashboardClient/ReportSection or passed through DashboardShell — and demote the existing section headings so the outline is h1 → h2 → h3 with no skipped levels.
references: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/info-and-relationships.html ; https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/headings-and-labels.html

title: "Click to browse" upload call-to-action fails AA text contrast (3.74:1)
evidence: `Click to browse or drag & drop PDFs` — text-teal-600 (#0d9488) on the white/slate-50 dropzone is 3.74:1. The text is text-sm (14px) font-medium, i.e. normal-size text, which requires >= 4.5:1.
impact: The primary affordance telling users they can click the dropzone is the lowest-contrast text on the screen; low-vision users may not perceive the actionable cue.
likelihood: Affects every low-vision user on the main upload surface.
remediation: Use teal-700 (#0f766e, 5.47:1 on white) for this text, matching the link color used elsewhere (e.g. dashboard-client.tsx:339).
references: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html

title: Worker loop drain/shutdown and slot-filling orchestration (loop.py) is untested
evidence:
Grep for loop/drain/graceful worker tests returns only the DB-primitive tests (test_worker_queue.py, test_reliability.py). The non-trivial orchestration is unexercised:
  if stop_event.is_set(): break            # stop claiming
  ...
  finally:
      if in_flight:
          await asyncio.gather(*in_flight, return_exceptions=True)   # drain
and _wait_for_capacity()/_install_signal_handlers() have no unit test.
impact: SECURITY.md and the worker docstring promise 'on SIGTERM it stops claiming and drains in-flight jobs before exiting, so docker compose down never strands a job.' That guarantee — plus the bounded-concurrency slot loop (len(in_flight) < concurrency) and once-per-poll recovery gating — has no test. A regression that exits without draining (stranding a claimed job in 'processing' until the stall timer) or busy-loops the claim while-loop would not be caught. The DB SKIP LOCKED claim is tested, but the loop that uses it is not.
likelihood: Medium — this is concurrency code that is easy to break subtly and the consequences (stranded/double-processed jobs) are exactly what the suite otherwise guards against.
remediation: Add unit tests with a fake ServiceContainer (no DB): assert that (a) with stop_event pre-set the loop drains a fake in-flight task via the finally gather; (b) the claim while-loop never exceeds WORKER_CONCURRENCY tasks; (c) _wait_for_capacity returns promptly when a slot frees or stop is set; (d) recovery runs at most once per poll interval. These need no Postgres.
references: CWE-1230 missing test for concurrency/shutdown invariant; reliability

title: Same-user concurrent duplicate uploads bypass in-flight dedup (TOCTOU) → two jobs, two extractions for identical bytes
evidence: route: `reusable = await container.job_service.create_reusable_job(...)` then later `job = await container.job_service.create_job(...)`. job_service.create_reusable_job: `inflight = await self.job_dao.find_active_duplicate(content_hash); if inflight is not None: return inflight; return None`. find_active_duplicate is a plain SELECT (`status.in_((PENDING_STATUS, ACTIVE_STATUS))`). Schema index is non-unique: Index("idx_jobs_owner_hash", "owner_id", "content_hash").
impact: Two requests from the same user uploading identical PDF bytes that arrive before either has committed its pending row both see no in-flight/cached duplicate and both create a pending job → the worker runs the extraction twice and bills OpenAI twice for the same document. The dedup/cache is documented as a cost guard, but it is racy because the check-then-insert is not atomic and there is no unique constraint to serialize it.
likelihood: Low–Medium — needs near-simultaneous identical uploads by one user (double-click, retried client, parallel automation). The per-user upload rate limit reduces but does not eliminate it (10/window still allows a tight burst).
remediation: Enforce dedup in the database rather than in application read-then-write: add a partial unique index (e.g. UNIQUE on (owner_id, content_hash) WHERE status IN ('pending','processing')) and handle the unique-violation by returning the existing active job; or perform the lookup+insert in a single transaction with appropriate locking. Treat the current path as best-effort and document it as such if not fixing.
references: CWE-367 (TOCTOU). The dedup intent is documented at job_service.py:100-112; best-effort nature is not surfaced to the cost guarantee.

title: Synchronous PDF retention sweep blocks the worker event loop, stalling all concurrent in-flight extractions
category: Performance / CWE-1050 (Excessive Platform Resource Consumption within a Loop)
impact: sweep_expired_pdfs (backend/app/worker/retention.py:42-49) does a synchronous Path.rglob("*.pdf") plus per-file entry.is_file()/entry.stat()/os.remove over the entire shared PDF volume. It is called directly on the worker's asyncio event loop, NOT via asyncio.to_thread (contrast with the PDF parse at extraction_service.py:116-121, which is correctly offloaded). With the new bounded-concurrency model (WORKER_CONCURRENCY default 4), up to 4 in-flight process_job coroutines are all blocked for the entire duration of the directory walk because the single-threaded loop cannot make progress. On a large/slow PHI volume the stall can be seconds-to-minutes, delaying every concurrent extraction's result write and its model-call awaits, and pushing jobs toward the stall-recovery timeout.
likelihood: Runs once per RETENTION_SWEEP_INTERVAL_SECONDS (default 3600s). Impact scales with the number of files on the volume and disk latency; deterministic, not a race.
remediation: Offload the sweep the same way the PDF parse is offloaded: await asyncio.to_thread(sweep_expired_pdfs, settings.PDF_MOUNT_PATH, settings.RETENTION_DAYS). Keep the last_sweep gating around the awaited call.
references: CWE-1050; consistency with extraction_service.py:116 (asyncio.to_thread for blocking pdfplumber)

title: Records report flattens, filters, and paginates entirely client-side over the loaded jobs' records[]
location: frontend/app/dashboard/report-section.tsx:39-45 (flattenReportRows/filter/summarize in useMemo); frontend/app/dashboard/records/records-client.tsx:7 (usePagedJobs(50)); frontend/lib/use-jobs.ts:164-177 (loadMore appends pages of full jobs)
evidence: `const rows = useMemo(() => flattenReportRows(jobs), [jobs]); ... filterReportRows(rows, filters); ... summarizeReportRows(filteredRows)`. usePagedJobs.loadMore appends full job objects (each with records[]) into one growing in-memory array: `setJobs((current) => [...current, ...page])`. The report flattens ALL loaded jobs into per-record rows, then slices PAGE_SIZE client-side.
impact: Each 'Load more' pulls another 50 full jobs (records[] included — see PERF list payload finding) into the browser, and every keystroke in the search box re-runs flatten+filter+summarize over the full flattened set. With a user who loads many pages, the in-memory ReportRow array and the per-keystroke recompute grow unbounded, causing input lag and high memory. Break point: thousands of flattened records make filtering janky and the cumulative payload large; there is no server-side record search/filter.
likelihood: Medium — only bites users who page deep into a large history and interact with filters, but degrades smoothly badly rather than failing.
remediation: Move record-level filtering/pagination server-side (a dedicated records endpoint returning flattened, paged records), or debounce the filter recompute and cap retained pages. Pair with the list-projection fix so loaded pages aren't carrying full result JSON when only record rows are needed.
references: Web performance: avoid O(n) recompute on every input; virtualization/server-side filtering

title: "· N flagged" job-card text fails AA text contrast (3.19:1)
evidence: `· {job.flagged.length} flagged` — text-amber-600 (#d97706) on white is 3.19:1. The surrounding metadata row is text-xs (12px), so this is normal-size text and must reach 4.5:1.
impact: The 'flagged' / needs-review count on each document row is hard to read for low-vision users — the very signal that should draw attention is under-contrast. (It is accompanied by the count text so it is not color-only, but it is below the contrast minimum.)
likelihood: Affects low-vision users on every document list row that has flagged records.
remediation: Use amber-700 (#b45309) which reaches 4.8:1 on white at this size, or render the count on an amber-50 chip.
references: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html

title: Cancelled status badge text fails AA contrast (4.40:1)
evidence: `cancelled: { label: "Cancelled", cls: "bg-zinc-100 text-zinc-500 ring-zinc-200", Icon: XCircle }` — text-zinc-500 (#71717a) on bg-zinc-100 (#f4f4f5) is 4.40:1. Badge text is 0.75rem (12px) font-weight 600, i.e. normal text requiring 4.5:1. All other status badges (completed/processing/pending/failed) use the *-700 text tokens and pass comfortably.
impact: The 'Cancelled' badge label is marginally below the contrast minimum for low-vision users, inconsistent with the other status badges.
likelihood: Low — applies only to cancelled jobs, and the shortfall is small.
remediation: Switch to text-zinc-600 (#52525b, ~6.5:1 on zinc-100) to match the *-700/600 pattern of the other badges.
references: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html

title: Job-detail page does not announce status changes during live polling
evidence:
The detail view polls every 2.5s while pending/processing (`const id = setInterval(() => void refresh(), 2500)`), updating the StatusBadge and re-rendering JobReport. Unlike the dashboard list — which has an sr-only live region (`

` in dashboard-client.tsx:298) — this page has no aria-live region. When a job transitions processing -> completed (or failed), nothing is announced; the StatusBadge text simply changes in place, which AT does not announce without a live region.
impact: A screen-reader user who opened a processing report and is waiting for it to finish receives no notification when extraction completes or fails; they must manually re-read the page to discover the new state.
likelihood: Occurs for every AT user who opens a still-processing job report (the common path immediately after upload).
remediation: Add an sr-only `role="status" aria-live="polite"` element that reflects the current job status (e.g. 'Extraction completed' / 'Extraction failed'), mirroring the pattern already used on the dashboard.
references: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/status-messages.html

title: No skip-to-content link to bypass the repeated header/nav blocks
location: frontend/components/dashboard-shell.tsx:47-112 (header + primary nav + mobile nav rendered before <main>); frontend/app/layout.tsx:22-29
evidence: Every authenticated page renders the DashboardShell header (logo, primary <nav>, on mobile a second <nav> row at line 92, user block, sign-out) immediately before `<main className="mx-auto max-w-6xl ...">{children}</main>` (line 112). There is no skip link and `<main>` has no id/tabindex target. layout.tsx wraps everything in ToastProvider with no skip link either.
impact: Keyboard and switch users must tab through the header and navigation blocks on every page load before reaching page content; there is no documented mechanism to bypass them.
likelihood: Low real-world friction (the nav block is small — two links plus sign-out) but it is a Level A requirement and applies on every page.
remediation: Add a visually-hidden-until-focused 'Skip to main content' link as the first focusable element (in layout.tsx or DashboardShell) targeting `<main id="main">`, and add `id="main"` to the <main> element.
references: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/bypass-blocks.html

title: Root package.json "test" script is a hard-coded failure stub
evidence: "scripts": { "test": "echo \"Error: no test specified\" && exit 1" }
impact: Running `npm test` at the repo root — the conventional entrypoint a reviewer/CI tries first — exits 1 with 'Error: no test specified', falsely implying the project has no tests. The real suites live in backend (pytest) and frontend (vitest) and are wired in .github/workflows/ci.yml, so CI is unaffected; but this is a misleading footgun for anyone running tests locally from the root and a default-npm-init leftover.
likelihood: Low impact (CI green, frontend job uses working-directory: frontend), but high likelihood of confusing a fresh evaluator.
remediation: Replace with a script that runs both suites (or at least points at them), e.g. "test": "cd frontend && npm test" plus a documented backend command, or remove the stub and document the per-package commands in README. At minimum make it not exit 1.
references: Test-harness hygiene

title: Summary aggregate's ::numeric cast on LLM-supplied JSON has no malformed-value test
evidence:
The aggregate hard-casts free-form JSON strings to numeric:
  COALESCE(SUM((rec->>'total_charges')::numeric), 0) AS total_charges
  ... CROSS JOIN LATERAL jsonb_array_elements(j.result->'records') AS rec
The only summary test (test_summary_aggregates_server_side_and_is_rls_scoped) seeds clean integer values; no test seeds a record where total_charges/balance is a non-numeric string (e.g. "1,200.00", "N/A", "").
impact: If the model ever emits a record whose total_charges is a non-numeric string (records are persisted as raw JSON; the value is a free-form float|null in the model but the DB stores whatever was written), (rec->>'total_charges')::numeric raises 'invalid input syntax for type numeric' and the WHOLE GET /jobs/summary request 500s for that user — a poison row breaks the entire dashboard summary. No test pins the behavior, so neither the failure nor a hardening fix is guarded.
likelihood: Low-to-medium — depends on extraction output discipline, but the cast is unguarded against a single bad value.
remediation: Add an integration test seeding a completed job whose result records include a non-numeric total_charges, asserting the desired contract (either it is skipped/coerced to 0, not a 500). If the current behavior is to 500, treat that as a bug and guard the cast (e.g. a safe to-numeric on rows matching '^-?[0-9.]+$', or NULLIF/regex filter) then lock it with the test.
references: CWE-20 Improper Input Validation; testing negative-path gap

title: No frontend test for lib/auth.ts production guardrails (DATABASE_URL reuse / missing-secret throws)
impact: The guardrails that stop the auth pool from connecting as the admin DB role in prod, and that refuse to boot without BETTER_AUTH_SECRET/URL, are unverified. A refactor that weakens these (e.g. drops the admin-URL equality check, or lets the dev placeholder secret leak into prod) would not be caught by CI. These are exactly the kind of fail-closed checks that warrant a regression test.
likelihood: Low — module-level env logic with import side effects is awkward to test, which is likely why it was skipped.
remediation: Extract the env-resolution/validation into a pure exported function (e.g. resolveAuthConfig(env)) and unit-test it: prod + AUTH_DATABASE_URL===DATABASE_URL throws; prod + missing secret throws; dev falls back to placeholders. Keeps the security contract pinned without import-time gymnastics.
references: OWASP A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration; CWE-1230 missing test for fail-closed config

title: Backend BETTER_AUTH_SECRET has an insecure built-in default and no production guard (asymmetric with the frontend)
category: OWASP A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures / A05 Security Misconfiguration; CWE-1188 (Insecure Default Initialization), CWE-798 (Hard-coded Credential), CWE-347 (Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature)
evidence: settings.py:27 -> `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET: str = "change_me_to_a_long_random_string"`  (no ENVIRONMENT/production guard anywhere; get_settings() at settings.py:130-132 just constructs Settings()). Consumed at auth.py:95 `raw_token = _extract_raw_token(request, settings.BETTER_AUTH_SECRET)` and used as the HMAC key in _unsign (auth.py:81-84). Contrast the frontend, which HARD-FAILS in production: frontend/lib/auth.ts:40-42 `if (!authSecret) { throw new Error("BETTER_AUTH_SECRET is required in production"); }`.
impact: If an operator deploys the API without setting BETTER_AUTH_SECRET, the backend silently boots in production (ENVIRONMENT defaults to 'production') using the publicly-known placeholder 'change_me_to_a_long_random_string' as its HMAC key, with no startup error and no warning. The frontend refuses to start in the same situation, so the two services disagree about whether the config is valid. The HMAC key only protects the signed-cookie / direct-API path (the no-dot bare-token path at auth.py:78-79 skips HMAC entirely, and the BFF forwards bare tokens). Crucially the DB session-table lookup (auth_dao.py:25-35) remains the real authority, so a weak HMAC key does NOT by itself yield an auth bypass: an attacker who knows the secret can forge a valid signature but still needs a raw token that exists in the high-entropy `session` table. Hence impact is limited to weakened defense-in-depth on the signed-value integrity check plus an inconsistent-config footgun, not a direct account-takeover.
likelihood: Low. Requires the operator to ignore SECURITY.md item 4 and .env.example guidance AND deploy the API with the secret unset/defaulted. Even then it is not directly exploitable without a valid session token, because the DB lookup gates access.
remediation: Mirror the frontend's fail-closed behavior on the backend: in Settings, reject the placeholder/default when ENVIRONMENT == 'production' (e.g. a pydantic validator that raises if BETTER_AUTH_SECRET is unset, equals 'change_me_to_a_long_random_string', or is shorter than 32 bytes when not in development). Drop the hard-coded default so an unconfigured production boot fails loudly instead of running with a known key.
references: OWASP A02:2021, A05:2021; CWE-1188, CWE-798, CWE-347; SECURITY.md:108-114 (Secrets management) already documents the intent, so code should enforce it. Compare frontend/lib/auth.ts:40-42.

title: Vision-fallback uploaded OpenAI file can be orphaned when the extraction wall-clock timeout cancels the run mid-flight
evidence: pdf_vision_extractor.run: `uploaded = await self.client.files.create(...); uploaded_id = uploaded.id; ... response = await self.client.responses.parse(...)` wrapped in `try/finally: if uploaded_id is not None: await self._delete_uploaded_file(uploaded_id, job_id)`. The whole run() is invoked under `asyncio.wait_for(operation(), timeout=self.extraction_timeout)` (extraction_service.py:261).
impact: When EXTRACTION_TIMEOUT_SECONDS elapses during responses.parse, asyncio.wait_for cancels the coroutine. The finally block then awaits files.delete during cancellation; an await issued while the task is being cancelled can itself be interrupted before the delete completes, leaving the uploaded PHI-bearing file resident on OpenAI's side. Over many timeouts this accumulates orphaned uploads (cost + a PHI-minimization gap), and a CancelledError raised here could also mask the original timeout. Note files.create/files.delete themselves have no explicit per-call timeout — they rely solely on the outer wall-clock bound.
likelihood: Low — only on the vision-fallback path and only when the model call exceeds the timeout. But that path is the more expensive/slow one, so timeouts there are not rare under load.
remediation: Make uploaded-file cleanup robust to cancellation: shield the delete (asyncio.shield) or schedule it via a non-cancellable cleanup, and/or run a periodic reconciliation that deletes stale OpenAI files by purpose. Add explicit timeouts to files.create/files.delete so cleanup itself cannot hang.
references: CWE-404 (improper resource shutdown/release); asyncio cancellation semantics in finally blocks.

title: Offset-based pagination in usePagedJobs.loadMore can skip or duplicate rows when the job list changes between pages
impact: The list is ordered created_at DESC (job_dao.list:189). loadMore uses offset: jobs.length against a server-side OFFSET (job_dao.list:192-194). If a new job is inserted (a fresh upload, or a completed cache-hit duplicate row created by create_reusable_job) between loading page 1 and pressing Load more, every existing row shifts down by one: the boundary row is re-fetched and appended a second time (duplicate React key `${job.job_id}` at reports.ts row id / rendered list), or, if a row is removed, one job is silently skipped. This is the classic OFFSET-pagination instability under concurrent mutation.
likelihood: Low-moderate — requires a list mutation during the (human-paced) window between page loads. usePagedJobs itself does not poll, which limits exposure, but uploads/cache-hit inserts are common on this surface.
remediation: This is the keyset-pagination item already documented as known-deferred in SECURITY.md; tracked as such. If/when addressed, page by a created_at+id cursor (WHERE (created_at,id) < (:last_created_at,:last_id)) instead of OFFSET. As a cheap interim guard, de-dupe on append by job_id before setJobs.
references: Known-deferred infra (keyset pagination) per audit context; offset-pagination drift

title: OFFSET-based pagination degrades on deep offsets (documents/records 'Load more')
evidence: `if offset: query = query.offset(offset)` with `query = select(Job).order_by(Job.created_at.desc())`. loadMore passes `offset: jobs.length`, so deep paging issues OFFSET 50,100,150,... Postgres still walks and discards all skipped rows even with the (owner_id, created_at DESC) index.
impact: For users with very large histories, late pages cost O(offset) row scans. Bounded by how deep a user actually pages, and the composite index keeps it index-only, so impact is modest. Already acknowledged as known-deferred (keyset pagination) in the task context/SECURITY.md.
likelihood: Low — requires deep manual paging; the index limits the damage.
remediation: Switch to keyset/seek pagination (WHERE created_at < :last_seen ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT n) using the existing composite index. Already noted as deferred infra.
references: Keyset pagination vs OFFSET; CWE-1050

title: Upload is fully buffered into memory before validation/persist; with worker pool/upload concurrency this multiplies RAM pressure
location: backend/app/api/routes/jobs.py:27-43 (_read_capped) and :71-90 (save); frontend/lib/api.ts:98-110 (uploadPdfs fires all files in parallel via Promise.all)
evidence: _read_capped accumulates `chunks: list[bytes]` and returns `b"".join(chunks)` — the whole file (up to MAX_UPLOAD_BYTES = 25 MB, MAX_UPLOAD_REQUEST_BYTES ~26 MB) is held in memory, then `data` is passed to validate/fingerprint/save. uploadPdfs: `await Promise.all(files.map(... uploadPdf ...))` uploads every selected file concurrently.
impact: The size cap (good — bounded, 413 on overflow) means a single request is bounded, but a batch drop of N PDFs uploads all N concurrently, and each in-flight request holds its full payload (plus the join doubling it transiently) in the API process. N concurrent 25 MB uploads = ~2N x 25 MB resident. On a small API container this is a memory-pressure / OOM risk under bursty multi-file uploads. Not a leak; a concurrency-multiplied buffer.
likelihood: Low — needs many large concurrent uploads; the per-request cap prevents the unbounded case.
remediation: Stream the upload to a temp file (or directly to storage) instead of joining all chunks into one bytes object, computing the SHA-256 incrementally; or cap client-side upload concurrency (e.g. a small pool) instead of Promise.all over the whole batch.
references: OWASP API4:2023 Unrestricted Resource Consumption

title: Retention rglob('*.pdf') over the whole volume runs synchronously on the worker event loop, blocking job claiming during the sweep
category: Performance / blocking sync I/O on event loop
location: backend/app/worker/loop.py:112-113 (sync call inside async loop) and backend/app/worker/retention.py:42-56 (list(root.rglob('*.pdf')) + per-file stat/unlink)
evidence: In the async `run()` loop: `sweep_expired_pdfs(settings.PDF_MOUNT_PATH, settings.RETENTION_DAYS)` is called without `await` / `run_in_executor`. Inside it: `entries = list(root.rglob("*.pdf"))` then a Python loop doing `entry.is_file()` / `entry.stat()` / `os.remove`.
impact: rglob materializes the entire recursive listing of the volume into a list, then stats every PDF, all on the event loop thread. While it runs, the worker cannot claim or advance in-flight jobs. With many owner subdirs and many files (and on a slow networked/volume FS) this can stall the worker for noticeable time each sweep interval. Mitigated by: jobs delete their own PDF on terminal state (so the directory should stay small) and the sweep is interval-gated. Break point: a volume that accumulates orphans (e.g. DELETE_PDF_AFTER_PROCESSING off) makes each sweep walk grow unbounded.
likelihood: Low — directory normally stays small; only bites if orphans accumulate or the FS is slow.
remediation: Offload the sweep to a thread via `await asyncio.get_running_loop().run_in_executor(None, sweep_expired_pdfs, ...)` so it never blocks claiming, and/or iterate lazily (os.scandir per owner dir) instead of materializing rglob.
references: asyncio: never run blocking I/O on the event loop

title: `test-results/` and `tmp/` are not gitignored
evidence: `.gitignore` ignores `data`, `.venv`, `.env*`, `backend/pdfs/*`, `*.zip`, etc., but has no `test-results/` or `tmp/` entry. `git check-ignore test-results` and `git check-ignore tmp` both return nothing. `test-results/.last-run.json` exists untracked-but-not-ignored at repo root.
impact: Test/eval artifact directories can be accidentally `git add`ed or swept into tooling that walks untracked files. The README's eval flow writes PHI-derived predictions under tmp/. Independent of the packager bug, these belong in .gitignore so they can never be committed.
likelihood: Low — requires an explicit add or a tool that consumes untracked files (see DATA-high).
remediation: Add `test-results/` and `tmp/` (and `tmp/predictions/`) to .gitignore.
references: CWE-200

title: ISC license declared but no LICENSE file and no third-party attribution/NOTICE
evidence: Root package.json declares `"license": "ISC"` but `git ls-files | grep -iE 'license|notice|copying'` returns nothing — there is no LICENSE file anywhere in the repo. frontend/package.json and backend/pyproject.toml declare no license field at all (grep -i 'license' returns empty for both). Dependencies span MIT/BSD/Apache-2.0/PSF (next, react, better-auth, pg, fastapi, pdfplumber→pdfminer.six, openai-agents, cryptography, etc.).
impact: A declared SPDX id with no accompanying license text is legally ambiguous and fails automated license/compliance scanners. No NOTICE/attribution file is provided for the Apache-2.0 and BSD components that request attribution. Frontend and backend sub-packages have no declared license, so their terms are undefined.
likelihood: Low — no copyleft (GPL/AGPL/MPL) was found in deps, so there is no contamination risk; this is a compliance/attribution gap, not a code defect.
remediation: Add a LICENSE file with the full ISC text (or whichever license is intended), set the `license` field consistently in frontend/package.json and backend/pyproject.toml, and add a NOTICE/THIRD-PARTY attribution file for Apache-2.0/BSD dependencies.
references: SPDX; npm 'license' field; Apache-2.0 §4(d)

title: billing_app role migration interpolates APP_DB_PASSWORD into SQL without escaping (unlike the auth-role migration)
category: Operations / SQL construction (CWE-89 SQL Injection — operator-controlled)
evidence: `APP_PASSWORD = os.environ.get("APP_DB_PASSWORD", "billing_app")` then `CREATE ROLE {APP_ROLE} LOGIN PASSWORD '{APP_PASSWORD}';` — the password is f-string-interpolated raw inside single quotes with no escaping or validation. By contrast the newer a6f2c9d8e3b1_auth_app_role.py:46-48 added `_literal()`/`_ident()` helpers that double single-quotes and validate identifiers. The hardened pattern was not back-applied here.
impact: A password containing a single quote (or SQL metacharacters) breaks the statement or could alter it. The value is operator-controlled at migration time (not user input), so this is a robustness/consistency gap rather than an exploitable injection; a secrets-manager-generated password with a quote would fail the migration.
likelihood: Low — depends on the chosen APP_DB_PASSWORD value; many generated secrets contain no quotes.
remediation: Reuse the `_literal()`/`_ident()` helpers from a6f2c9d8e3b1 to quote the password and role name in a1771a9c2e01 as well.
references: CWE-89

title: Every DB session issues a separate SET-config round-trip before its query (extra round-trip per read)
location: backend/app/core/context_manager.py:64-86 (_apply_identity executes a standalone SELECT set_config before yielding); backend/app/dao/pg/job_dao.py:148-151, 188-197, 211-252 (each method opens its own session)
evidence: session(): `await self._apply_identity(session)` runs `await session.execute(text("SELECT set_config(:name, :value, true)"), ...)` as a discrete statement before the actual query; each DAO method (`update_status`, `list`, `summary` which opens one session for two queries, `find_cached_result`, etc.) opens its own `context_manager.session()`.
impact: Adds one network round-trip per logical DB operation for the identity GUC, and operations that could share a transaction (e.g. cancel_job's get + cancel + get is three sessions = three set_config round-trips) instead pay it repeatedly. At low scale negligible; at high request rate it doubles round-trips on simple reads and consumes pool capacity (pool_size 5 + overflow 10) faster.
likelihood: Low — per-call cost is small; only matters under high QPS or when many short ops chain.
remediation: Optional: batch set_config into the first real statement, or hold one session per request across the service operations that belong to the same logical action, so identity is set once per transaction rather than per DAO call.
references: DB round-trip minimization; connection-pool sizing
